stem: What explains the mathematical forgetting curve?
explanation: Jost's law is the formal statement that of two equally strong memories the older one decays more slowly, which is what gives the forgetting curve its decelerating (roughly power/logarithmic) shape. The distractors are empirical effects, not accounts of the curve's mathematical form.

stem: Lansdale and Baguley (2008) predict that the probability of correct recall depends on
explanation: Lansdale and Baguley's fragmentation account treats a memory as a set of traces, so the probability of correct recall is a function of the proportion of correct traces available at test. The other options name principles about cues and interference rather than a quantity that predicts recall probability.

stem: The study of how the ‘War of the Ghosts’ story accounts became altered at recall was carried out by
explanation: The 'War of the Ghosts' study is Frederic Bartlett (1932), Remembering, the classic demonstration that recall is reconstructive and shaped by schemas. Godden and Baddeley (1975) is the diver study on context-dependent memory, a common distractor here.

stem: Eyewitness testimonies are an example of what kind of memory experiment?
explanation: Witnesses do not know in advance that they will be tested, so they never intend to memorise the event. Learning without intent to learn is incidental learning; intentional learning (A) is the trap, since it describes a lab participant deliberately told to memorise a list.

stem: Which key academic is involved in the study of memory as a reconstructive process?
explanation: Elizabeth Loftus is the name attached to reconstructive memory and the misinformation effect (leading questions altering what witnesses 'remember'). Baddeley and Hitch are the working-memory model, not reconstruction.

stem: The accuracy of eyewitness testimonies can be improved with
explanation: The cognitive interview (Fisher and Geiselman) is the standard evidence-based technique for improving eyewitness accuracy: report everything, reinstate context, change order, change perspective. Recovered memories (A) are the opposite, they are associated with false memory, not accuracy.

stem: What can help improve the accuracy of eye witness testimonies?
explanation: Context reinstatement, mentally putting yourself back at the scene, is a core component of the cognitive interview and reliably boosts recall by restoring retrieval cues. Mnemonics and distributed practice are study techniques for deliberate learning, not tools for recovering an unrehearsed witnessed event.

stem: Memory can be improved when information to be learnt is
explanation: Organising material (hierarchies, categories) gives recall a retrieval structure and reliably improves memory. Note the item is weak: elaboration (D) is also a genuine memory booster, so both A and D are defensible; the key is A.

stem: Which is a good method of revision:
explanation: Spaced (distributed) learning beats massed cramming: the same total study time split across separated sessions produces markedly better long-term retention. Intense single sessions and late-night cramming are the classic wrong answers.

stem: How many address bits are needed to select all memory locations in the 2118 16K × 1 RAM?
explanation: 16K = 16 x 1024 = 16384 locations, and 16384 = 2^14, so 14 address bits are needed. The trap is 16, which comes from reading the '16' in '16K' rather than computing the power of two.

stem: The check sum method of testing a ROM:

options.label: A
options.text: indicates if the data in more than one memory location is incorrect.

options.label: B
options.text: provides a means for locating and correcting data errors in specific memory locations.

options.label: C
options.text: allows data errors to be pinpointed to a specific memory location.

options.label: D
options.text: simply indicates that the contents of the ROM are incorrect.

explanation: A checksum sums the ROM's contents and compares the total against a stored expected value, so a mismatch tells you only that something in the ROM is wrong. It cannot say which location failed and cannot correct it, and it can even miss errors that cancel out in the sum.

stem: Refer to the given figures (a) and (b). A logic analyzer is used to check the circuit in figure (a) and displays the waveforms shown in figure (b). The actual analyzer display shows all four data outputs, Q0-Q3. The analyzer's cursor is placed at position X and all four of the data output lines show a Low-level output. What is wrong, if anything, with the circuit?

options.label: A
options.text: Nothing is wrong, according to the display. The outputs are in the open state and should show zero output voltage.

options.label: B
options.text: The circuit is in the READ mode and the outputs, Q0-Q3, should reflect the contents of the memory at that address. The chip is defective; replace the chip.

options.label: C
options.text: The circuit is in the WRITE mode and should be writing the contents of the selected address to Q0–Q3.

options.label: D
options.text: The Q0–Q3 lines can be either LOW or HIGH, since the chip is in the tristate mode in which case their level is unpredictable.

explanation: At cursor X the timing diagram has the RAM in write mode with its three-state outputs disabled, so Q0-Q3 are in the open (high-impedance) state and the analyzer reads them as zero volts, i.e. LOW. Nothing is broken. Choice B is the trap: it assumes the chip is in READ mode, which the control lines at X do not show.

stem: What is the meaning of RAM, and what is its primary role?

options.label: A
options.text: Readily Available Memory; it is the first level of memory used by the computer in all of its operations.

options.label: B
options.text: Random Access Memory; it is memory that can be reached by any sub-system within a computer, and at any time.

options.label: C
options.text: Random Access Memory; it is the memory used for short-term temporary data storage within the computer.

options.label: D
options.text: Resettable Automatic Memory; it is memory that can be used and then automatically reset, or cleared, after being read from or written to.

explanation: RAM is Random Access Memory: volatile read/write memory used as the computer's short-term working store for the code and data currently in use. B is the trap, 'random access' refers to the ability to reach any address in constant time, not to which sub-systems may access it.

stem: The storage element for a static RAM is the ________.
explanation: A static RAM cell stores its bit in a latch (flip-flop), which holds state as long as power is applied and needs no refresh. The capacitor is the DRAM storage element, and its charge leaks away, which is exactly why DRAM must be refreshed.

stem: In a DRAM, what is the state of R/W during a read operation?
explanation: The line is R/W-bar: HIGH selects read, LOW selects write (the bar applies to the write half). So during a read it is HIGH. LOW is the trap for anyone who forgets the active-low convention on the W.

stem: The condition occurring when two or more devices try to write data to a bus simultaneously is called ________.
explanation: Two or more devices driving the same bus lines at the same time is bus contention; it is prevented by three-state outputs and address decoding that enable only one driver at a time. Address decoding and multiplexing are the mechanisms that avoid the problem, not names for it.

stem: For the given circuit, what memory location is being addressed?
explanation: The address lines in the figure give A7..A0 = 11111001. Decoded as row/column: rows from A4-A0 = 11001 = 25 and columns from A7-A5 = 111 = 7, so the location is 7 x 32 + 25 = 249, which is just binary 11111001 read as a decimal number.

stem: A command that takes what has been typed into the computer and can be seen on the screen and sends it to the printer for output on paper______.
explanation: Straight definition item: the print command sends what is on screen or typed to the printer for output on paper. No reasoning behind it.

stem: RAM stands for :
explanation: RAM stands for Random Access Memory, meaning any address can be reached in roughly the same time regardless of its position. 'Read Access Memory' is the invented distractor.

stem: _______ tags, when placed on an animal, can be used to record and track in a database all of the animal’s movements.
explanation: RFID (Radio Frequency Identification) tags carry a chip and antenna that a reader energises by radio, so each time the tagged animal passes a reader its ID and timestamp are logged to a database. GPS is a positioning system, not a tag read by a scanner, and POS/PPS are not tags at all.

stem: Chip is a common nickname for a (n):
explanation: Answer unverified: the source PDF marks no key for this item. 'Chip' is the everyday nickname for an integrated circuit, a silicon die packing many transistors, so C is the intended answer. Transistor and resistor are individual components on the chip, and semiconductor is the material (silicon) the chip is made from, which is the classic trap here.

stem: All of the following are examples of storage devices except:
explanation: Storage devices retain data (hard disk, USB flash drive, DVD drive), while a printer only produces output on paper and holds nothing for later retrieval, so the printer is the exception.

stem: ____ is any part of the computer that you can physically touch.
explanation: Hardware is by definition every physical, touchable part of the computer. Device and peripheral are narrower subsets (a peripheral is only the external hardware), and an application is software, which has no physical form.

stem: A keyboard is this kind of device—
explanation: A keyboard sends keystrokes into the computer, so it is an input device. Direction of data flow is the whole test: into the machine is input, out of it (monitor, printer, speaker) is output.

stem: The ______, also called the “brains” of the computer, is responsible for processing data.
explanation: The CPU executes instructions and performs arithmetic and logic, which is why it is called the brains of the computer. The motherboard is only the board it sits on, and RAM/memory just holds the data and instructions the CPU works on.
